text: The surgeon creates a wide peritoneal flap to expose a large indirect hernia defect in the right lower quadrant. The defect is visible, and the surgical setup resembles that of an inguinal hernia repair.
The surgeon identifies a true obturator hernia on the patient's side, noting the presence of a true peritoneal sac. The hernia is planned to be covered with mesh, alongside a visible fat plug.

The surgeon reduces the entire hernia sac, exposing the round ligament. The peritoneum is carefully dissected away from the ligament to allow for safe cauterization and division. The right medial preperitoneal space is then parietalized.

The surgeon examines the right obturator space, revealing a hernia defect with a visible peritoneal sac extending into the space. The surgeon prepares to reduce the hernia, noting the presence of a true peritoneal sac rather than the typical fat plug.

The surgeon completes the preparation for mesh placement, inspecting the surgical field for a lipoma. A small amount of fat is visible entering the indirect space, providing a clear view of the widened indirect hernia defect.
The surgeon places a 15x15 ProGrip mesh over the hernia defect, ensuring coverage of the obturator space. The flap is then closed to secure the mesh in position.
